# Preface {.unnumbered}

This is the **panproto Developer Guide** --- an architecture reference and contributor handbook for the panproto project. It documents the internals of the universal schema migration engine: how crates are organized, how the algebraic machinery works under the hood, and how to contribute effectively.

## Who this guide is for

This guide is written for three audiences:

- **Contributors** submitting bug fixes, performance improvements, or new features to the panproto codebase.
- **Protocol authors** adding support for new schema languages (e.g., a new IDL or data-definition format) by implementing panproto's protocol trait.
- **Researchers** who want to understand panproto's internals --- its use of Generalized Algebraic Theories, W-type instances, and bidirectional lenses --- in order to extend or build on the engine.

If you are a *user* of panproto who wants to learn how to define schemas, run migrations, and use the TypeScript SDK, start with the [panproto Tutorial](https://panproto.github.io/tutorial/) instead. The tutorial teaches you how to **use** panproto; this guide explains how it **works**.

## Prerequisites

You should be comfortable with:

- **Rust** (ownership, traits, generics, `Result`-based error handling). The codebase targets Edition 2024.
- **Basic TypeScript**, enough to read the SDK wrapper and its tests.
- **Algebraic concepts** like sorts, operations, and equations. You do not need a category theory background --- the [tutorial's category theory cheatsheet](https://panproto.github.io/tutorial/appendices/A-category-theory-cheatsheet.html) provides a gentle introduction --- but familiarity with algebraic thinking will help.

## How to navigate this guide

The guide is organized into five parts:

**Part I --- Getting Started.**
Read @sec-welcome and @sec-first-contribution to understand the project's goals, clone the repository, and land your first pull request. This is the recommended starting point for all new contributors.

**Part II --- Development Environment.**
Chapters 3--4 cover building, testing, debugging, and profiling. Return here whenever you need to set up a new machine or diagnose a test failure.

**Part III --- Architecture & Internals.**
Chapters 5--15 are deep dives into each crate and subsystem. Read the architecture overview first, then jump to whichever crate you are working on.

**Part IV --- Contributing Process.**
Chapters 16--18 cover coding conventions, the testing philosophy, and the PR workflow. Read these before opening your first pull request.

**Part V --- Project Operations.**
Chapter 19 covers the release process and versioning policy.

**Appendices.**
The glossary (Appendix A) defines every domain term. The source code map (Appendix B) is a directory-level index of the entire repository. The troubleshooting FAQ (Appendix C) addresses common build and test issues.

::: {.callout-tip}
## Quick-start path

If you want to land a contribution as fast as possible: read chapters 1 and 2, build the project (chapter 3), make your change, then read chapters 16--18 before opening your PR.
:::

## Links

- **Tutorial:** <https://panproto.github.io/tutorial/>
- **Repository:** <https://github.com/panproto/panproto>
- **Rustdoc:** generated from `cargo doc --workspace --no-deps`

## Conventions used in this guide

- Inline code like `panproto-gat` refers to a crate name.
- File paths like `crates/panproto-gat/src/theory.rs` are relative to the repository root.
- Shell commands assume a Unix-like environment (macOS or Linux).
- Callout boxes highlight important notes, tips, and warnings.

---

*Aaron Steven White --- {{{< meta date >}}}*
